content.theme: You are the wall. You stand between your crew and everything that wants them dead. You absorb damage, redirect attacks, and punish anyone stupid enough to hit you. Other classes avoid getting hit. You plan on it.
content.design: The Fortress is the tank. Two branches — Guardian (protect allies: intercept attacks, shield teammates, zone control) and Juggernaut (punish attackers: thorns damage, counter-attacks, escalating power when hurt) — share the same tree and activate simultaneously when conditions overlap. The Fortress solves the core tactical tank problem — forcing enemies to deal with you instead of your squishier allies — through threat generation, opportunity attacks, and positional control.

content.label: Critical Rule
items: The Fortress trades offense for durability and presence. You deal less damage than any DPS class. Your accuracy is mediocre (Chrome for melee, not Reflex or Interface). Your contribution is measured in damage your allies DIDN'T take because you were standing in the way. A fight where the Fortress takes 80% of all incoming damage and nobody else takes a Scar is a fight the Fortress won.

content.label: Key Differences
items: vs Fury: Fury is an aggressive melee class that takes damage as a side effect of being in the fight. Fortress is a defensive melee class that takes damage ON PURPOSE. Fury wants to kill things fast so they stop hitting back. Fortress wants things to keep hitting them because every hit triggers augments.

vs Sin Eater: Sin Eater absorbs conditions (Bleed, Poison, Stagger). Fortress absorbs raw damage (HP loss). Sin Eater manages Toxicity as their resource. Fortress manages HP and positioning as theirs. Sin Eater is fragile and stays at range. Fortress is the hardest thing to kill in the game and stands in melee.

vs Technician: Technician controls the map with objects (turrets, mines, barriers). Fortress controls the map with their body. A Technician's barrier can be destroyed from range. The Fortress IS the barrier — and they hit back.

content.text: Core Mechanics
Guardian Trigger

When an ally within Close range is targeted by an attack or takes damage. Guardian augments activate when you protect others. Intercept redirects attacks to you. Shield augments extend your armor to allies. Zone control makes enemies pay for ignoring you. The Guardian is only as good as their positioning — if allies aren't near you, your triggers don't fire.
Juggernaut Trigger

When you take damage from any source. Juggernaut augments activate when YOU get hit. Thorns damage fires back at attackers. Counter-attack options open up. Damage bonuses escalate the more hurt you are. The Juggernaut wants to be the center of attention — every hit makes them stronger. Enemies who ignore the Juggernaut and target allies are making the right tactical call. The Fortress's job is to make that impossible.
Combined

Enemy attacks your ally → you use Intercept (Guardian — redirect attack to yourself) → you take damage (Juggernaut — thorns damage fires back, counter-attack available) → ally is unharmed, attacker took damage, you gained Juggernaut bonuses. Both branches activated from one enemy action. The Fortress who invests in both is a complete tank — protecting AND punishing simultaneously.
Threat Generation
The Fortress needs enemies to attack them. Without threat mechanics, smart enemies (or video game AI) just walk around the tank and kill the Channeler. Fortress threat generation works through:
Opportunity Attacks: Enemies that move away from you or move past you provoke a free melee attack. Standard rule, but Fortress augments make these hit harder and add conditions.
Taunt: Active ability that forces an enemy to target you. Not optional — the target MUST attack you on their next turn if able.

Zone of Control: The Fortress's adjacent tiles are contested. Enemies entering or passing through pay a cost (reduced movement, opportunity attack, accuracy penalty). You control space by standing in it.
Punishment for Ignoring: Several augments trigger when an enemy within Close range attacks someone other than you. They don't HAVE to attack you — but it costs them if they don't.

content.label: Natural Counters
items: Ranged enemies at Long range — outside your Close range zone, can't Intercept or threaten them. You need the squad to close distance or advance under fire.
Kiting / mobile enemies — enemies that attack and retreat stay out of your zone. You're slow (heavy armor, probably not investing in Reflex).
Armor penetration — your defense is high armor. Enemies with Pen or armor-ignoring damage (rift energy, acid) bypass your primary defense. High HP compensates, but you feel it.
Crowd control — Stun, Immobilize, or forced movement that displaces you from allies removes Guardian triggers. A stunned Fortress is a squad with no protection.
Splitting the party — if allies spread across the map, you can only protect the ones near you. AoE-heavy encounters that force scatter reduce your effectiveness.
Being ignored — if enemies are smart enough to just not attack you, Juggernaut triggers don't fire. Taunt and punishment mechanics exist for this, but they have cooldowns and AP costs.

augments.name: Subdermal Plating
augments.effect: +1 Armor (stacks with worn armor). The foundation. Simple, essential.

augments.name: Guardian Protocol
augments.effect: Adjacent allies gain +1 Armor. Your physical presence provides cover.

augments.name: Immovable Frame
augments.effect: You cannot be forcibly moved by enemy abilities (push, pull, knockback). +2 to resist knockdown. You stay where you plant yourself.

augments.name: Reactive Plating
augments.effect: When hit by a melee attack, the attacker takes 1 damage (armor-ignoring). Your plating bites back. Passive, automatic, every hit.

augments.name: Shock Absorbers
augments.effect: Reduce the first instance of damage you take each round by 2 (after armor). One free buffer per round.

augments.name: Sentinel Reflexes
augments.effect: +1 RP per round. Your augmented reflexes let you react faster and more often. Stacks with baseline 2 RP.

augments.name: Intercept
augments.effect: Reaction: when an ally within Close range is targeted by an attack, you can redirect the attack to yourself. The attack is resolved against YOUR Evasion and Armor instead. Once per round. The core Guardian mechanic — you step in front of the bullet.

augments.name: Shield Wall
augments.effect: While you are adjacent to an ally and haven't moved this turn, that ally gains +2 Evasion (you provide heavy cover). Multiple allies can benefit if they're all adjacent. Positional reward.

augments.name: Anchored Defense
augments.effect: While you haven't moved this turn, enemies cannot move through your tile or tiles adjacent to you without spending +1 AP per tile. You are terrain. Your body blocks the path.

augments.name: Hardened Frame
augments.effect: +4 maximum HP. Simple scaling. You just have more to spend.

augments.name: Thorns Plating
augments.effect: When hit by any attack (melee or ranged), the attacker takes 2 damage (armor-ignoring). Upgrade from Reactive Plating — now works on ranged attacks too. Stacks with Reactive Plating for 3 total on melee.

augments.name: Pain Fuel
augments.effect: When you take damage, your next melee attack this turn deals +2 bonus damage. Getting hit makes you hit harder. Resets at end of turn.

augments.name: Ablative Shielding
augments.effect: The first attack against you each encounter deals 0 damage (completely absorbed). One-time buffer per fight.

augments.name: Extended Guard
augments.effect: Intercept range increases from Close (adjacent) to Close range (within 10ft). Protect allies from further away.

augments.name: Guardian's Reprisal
augments.effect: When you use Intercept to absorb an attack meant for an ally, you may immediately make a free melee attack against the attacker (if they're within melee range). Protect AND punish in the same action.

augments.name: Fortress Wall
augments.effect: +2 RP per round. Your Opportunity Attacks cost 1 less RP (minimum 1). Combined with Sentinel Reflexes, you have 5 RP/round and can threaten OAs with any weapon in the game.

augments.name: Punishment Field
augments.effect: When an enemy within Close range attacks anyone other than you, they take -2 accuracy on that attack. You are so threatening that ignoring you is distracting. Passive, always active.

augments.name: Iron Escalation
augments.effect: Track total damage taken this encounter. At 10+ damage taken: +1 to all melee attack rolls. At 20+ damage taken: +2 to all melee attack rolls and +1 damage per die. At 30+ damage taken: +3 to all melee attack rolls and +2 damage per die. The more they hurt you, the more dangerous you become.

augments.name: Kinetic Dispersal
augments.effect: Reduce all AoE damage you take by half (rounded down). Grenades, Rift Storms, explosions — you tank through them.

augments.name: Counter-Strike
augments.effect: When you are hit by a melee attack, you may spend your Reaction to make an immediate melee attack against the attacker. Once per round. They swing, you swing back.

augments.name: Damage Conversion
augments.effect: Convert the first 3 damage you take each round into Toxicity instead (1 Toxicity per 2 damage, round up). Your augments absorb kinetic energy and convert it to rift contamination. Delays HP loss but builds Toxicity. Dangerous for long fights. (Trade-off augment.)

augments.name: Fortress Aura
augments.effect: All allies within Close range gain +1 Armor and +1 Evasion. Passive aura. Your presence hardens the squad.

augments.name: Mass Intercept
augments.effect: Once per encounter: when an AoE effect targets your allies within Close range, you can absorb the entire effect. All allies in the AoE take 0 damage. You take the full damage for each ally who would have been hit (damage × number of allies protected). Heroic and potentially suicidal.

augments.name: Last Stand
augments.effect: Once per mission: when you would drop to 0 HP, you stay at 1 HP instead. For 2 rounds after triggering: +2 Armor, +2 to all melee attacks, immune to conditions. When the 2 rounds end, you drop to 0 HP and gain a Scar. You get your hero moment, then you pay for it.

augments.name: Siege Mode
augments.effect: 1 AP activation, lasts until you move or deactivate (free action). While active: +3 Armor, cannot be flanked, opportunity attacks hit all enemies that move within Close range (not just adjacent), all melee attacks deal +1d4 bonus damage. Cannot move while active. You ARE the fortification.

augments.name: Iron Will
augments.effect: +2 to all Scar survival rolls. You can carry 2 additional Scars before forced retirement. Your body endures what others can't. The long-game tank augment — you'll take more Scars than anyone, so you need more capacity.

augments.name: Unbreakable
augments.effect: Passive. Maximum Scar count increased by 2 (stacks with Iron Will for +4 total). You cannot be killed by a single attack — any attack that would reduce you from above 50% HP to 0 HP instead leaves you at 1 HP (once per encounter, separate from Last Stand). Intercept costs no Reaction (can Intercept every attack against adjacent allies, unlimited per round). All damage you take is reduced by 2 (flat, after armor, stacks with Shock Absorbers). Allies within Close range cannot be critically hit. You are the immovable object. Everything breaks against you.

content.text: Synergy Examples
Pure Guardian Build (The Wall)

Subdermal Plating → Guardian Protocol → Intercept → Shield Wall → Anchored Defense → Extended Guard → Fortress Wall → Fortress Aura → Mass Intercept. Every turn: stand in front of the squad. Don't move (Shield Wall + Brace = allies get +2 Evasion + you get +2 Armor). Intercept any attack against adjacent allies. Fortress Wall punishes enemies who try to walk past. Anchored Defense costs them extra AP to get around you. Mass Intercept is the emergency — absorb an entire AoE for the squad. Your personal damage output is near zero. Your squad's survival is near perfect. You go home with Scars so they don't.
Pure Juggernaut Build (The Punching Bag)

Subdermal Plating → Reactive Plating → Shock Absorbers → Thorns Plating → Pain Fuel → Iron Escalation → Counter-Strike → Last Stand → Siege Mode. Every turn: walk into the biggest cluster of enemies and dare them to hit you. Every melee hit triggers Thorns (2 damage) + Reactive Plating (+1 damage) + Counter-Strike (free melee attack). Pain Fuel makes your attacks hit harder each time you're hit. Iron Escalation scales your damage with total damage taken. By round 4 at 20+ damage absorbed, you're hitting at +2 accuracy and +1/die — competitive with actual DPS classes. Siege Mode locks you in place with +3 Armor and enhanced opportunity attacks. Last Stand gives you a final stand moment. You're not protecting anyone — you're daring the enemy to kill you and making them regret trying.
Hybrid Build (The Complete Tank)

Subdermal Plating → Guardian Protocol → Intercept → Reactive Plating → Thorns Plating → Guardian's Reprisal → Iron Escalation → Fortress Aura → Last Stand. The full package. Intercept redirects attacks to you (Guardian) → Thorns fires back at the attacker (Juggernaut) → Guardian's Reprisal gives you a free melee swing at the attacker → Iron Escalation tracks damage taken for scaling bonuses. Every Intercept triggers both branches simultaneously. Fortress Aura passively buffs the squad. Last Stand is the emergency. You protect AND punish. The trade-off: spread across both branches, so neither is at full depth.
Fortress + Sin Eater Cross-Tree

Fortress T2 (Subdermal Plating, Intercept) + Sin Eater T2 (Empathic Link, Sympathetic Healing). The immortal healer-tank. You Intercept attacks (taking damage instead of allies). Sin Eater Absorbs conditions off allies. Between the two, you're handling all incoming physical damage AND all incoming conditions. High armor means the physical damage hurts less. The problem: you're splitting augment investment AND stat investment (Chrome/Grit vs Interface/Edge). You do both jobs adequately, neither expertly.
Fortress + Fury Cross-Tree

Fortress T2 (Subdermal Plating, Reactive Plating) + Fury T2 (Adrenal Injector, rage bonuses). The aggressive tank. Fury augments reward taking damage with offensive bonuses. Fortress augments ensure you survive the damage. You charge in, tank hits, deal increasing damage as Fury builds, and Reactive Plating punishes attackers. The problem: Fury wants to be reckless (big swings, exposed positioning). Fortress wants to be positioned (stay near allies, hold chokepoints). The playstyles pull in different directions.
Fortress + Precision Cross-Tree

Fortress T2 (Subdermal Plating, Guardian Protocol) + Precision T2 (Targeting Suite, Marksman bonuses). The ranged tank. Stand in front of the squad wearing heavy armor. Don't move (Brace + Shield Wall for allies). Shoot with rifle (Marksman bonus for not moving). You're the front line AND a ranged damage dealer — enemies have to get through you to reach the squad, and you're shooting them the whole time. The problem: Chrome/Grit doesn't boost ranged accuracy (that's Reflex/Edge). Your shots are mediocre. But you're the only “tank” who contributes damage from a distance.
Fortress + Technician Cross-Tree

Fortress T2 (Subdermal Plating, Immovable Frame) + Technician T2 (Deployable Sync, Fortified Construction). The bunker. Place a turret behind you. Place a Barrier Generator beside you. Stand in front. You are the wall, the turret is the gun, the barrier extends your cover. Enemies that reach you face opportunity attacks (Hold the Line), thorns damage (Reactive Plating), AND turret fire. The problem: immobile. If the fight moves, your entire setup is stranded.
Design Notes

Why Guardian vs Juggernaut instead of Offense vs Defense: Both branches are defensive in nature — the Fortress doesn't have an “offensive” identity. The split is about WHO benefits from your tanking. Guardian protects the squad (Intercept, Shield Wall, Fortress Aura). Juggernaut protects yourself and punishes attackers (thorns, counter-attacks, escalation). Both make the Fortress harder to ignore. Guardian does it by being the only thing between enemies and your allies. Juggernaut does it by being actively dangerous to attack.

Why Intercept is T2 instead of T1: Intercept is the Guardian's core mechanic — the ability to redirect attacks from allies to yourself. Making it T2 (requiring 1 augment first) means the Bulwark has to buy Subdermal Plating or Guardian Protocol first. This is intentional: you need to be tough enough to absorb hits before you start volunteering for them. A Fortress who Intercepts at T1 with no armor bonus is just dying faster.

Why Hold the Line stops movement: The fundamental tank problem in tactical games is that enemies walk around you. Hold the Line says “no.” Enemies adjacent to you cannot leave without taking an opportunity attack that STOPS their movement — they don't just take damage and keep going, they're stuck. This makes the Fortress a genuine obstacle.

Why Iron Escalation tracks total damage, not current HP: Tracking total damage taken (cumulative, not current) means healing doesn't reset your bonuses. A Fortress at Iron Escalation tier 2 (+20 damage taken) who gets healed to full HP still has their +2 accuracy and +1 damage/die. The investment is permanent for the encounter. This prevents the feel-bad of “I got healed so now I'm weaker.” Your suffering is banked.

Why Last Stand has a delayed Scar: Last Stand gives you 2 rounds of invulnerability followed by automatic 0 HP and a Scar. This is a known cost — you're not gambling on survival, you're buying exactly 2 more rounds and paying exactly 1 Scar. Do you pop Last Stand to finish the fight, knowing it costs you a Scar? Sometimes yes. Sometimes you let yourself go down and save the Scar.

Why Fortress Aura is T4: +1 Armor and +1 Evasion to all allies within Close range is one of the strongest passive effects in the game. At T4, the Fortress has invested 6+ augments into the tree. They've earned the right to make the entire squad tougher just by existing. Earlier access would be too strong.

Why Unbreakable prevents one-shot kills: The T5 mastery includes “any attack that would reduce you from above 50% HP to 0 HP instead leaves you at 1 HP.” This prevents the feel-bad of investing 10 augments into being tough and then dying to a single Cataclysm. You can still be killed by two attacks, or by chip damage — but nothing one-shots the Fortress at T5. This is the reward for maximum tank investment.

Why the Fortress has low personal damage: This is the hardest class to balance because “unkillable + high damage” is broken. The Fortress's melee damage is adequate (Precision Blade does fine) but they have no damage augments — no accuracy bonuses, no damage dice upgrades, no penetration. Pain Fuel and Iron Escalation provide conditional damage boosts (only when taking damage), but the Fortress will never out-DPS a Shadow, Precision, or Channeler. If you want a tank that also deals damage, cross-tree into Fury — but you sacrifice tank depth for offense.

content.label: AP Math — Typical Fortress Turns (5 AP)
items: The Wall: Don't move (Shield Wall + Brace active) → Taunt priority target (1 AP) → Melee attack (2 AP) → Melee attack (2 AP). Net: 2 attacks, 1 enemy forced to hit you, allies behind you get +2 Evasion and +2 Armor from Shield Wall + Brace.
Engage: Bulwark Rush (2 AP, move + attack + push) → Taunt (1 AP) → Melee attack (2 AP). Net: closed distance, 2 attacks, 1 enemy taunted. Now in position for next turn's Shield Wall.

Full Guardian: Intercept (Reaction, free) → Bodyguard Stance on Channeler (1 AP) → Taunt on biggest enemy (1 AP) → Melee attack (2 AP) → Brace (passive, didn't move). Net: Channeler completely protected, 1 enemy taunted, 1 attack dealt, high armor.

Emergency: War Cry (2 AP, mass Taunt + ally accuracy) → Recover (2 AP, heal 1d6 + Grit) → Shield Bash (1 AP, push enemy away from downed ally). Net: all enemies targeting you, small heal, repositioned 1 enemy. Squad stabilized.

Siege: Already in Siege Mode (activated last turn). Melee attack (2 AP) → Melee attack (2 AP) → Taunt (1 AP). +3 Armor, can't be flanked, opportunity attacks hit everything that moves near. You are a turret made of meat and chrome.
